nodes.body: Returning successfully when Web3j is unconfigured makes the Kafka listener treat the message as processed, so offsets can be committed while the event is never anchored. If the service starts before `web3j.private-key` or `web3j.contract-address` is supplied, it can drain the topic and permanently lose those blockchain writes; consider disabling consumption until configured or routing these events to a retry/DLQ path instead of no-oping.

Swallowing `BlockchainWriteException` after the retry loop means Spring Kafka will consider the record handled and advance the offset, so any transient Ganache/RPC outage longer than the three in-process retries results in a permanently missing audit anchor. To preserve audit completeness while still avoiding partition stalls, route failed records to a retry topic/DLQ or use an explicit acknowledgement strategy instead of committing them as success.

This checklist marks the Testcontainers subtask complete, but the new audit-writer tests are Mockito/unit tests and the test tree contains no Testcontainers usage. Either add the Kafka/Ganache integration coverage required by the subtask or leave this item unchecked so the plan reflects the actual test coverage.

The test comment says the retry delay is overridden to 0 ms, but the subclass does not override any delay behavior (`sleep` is private), so this test still waits for the production retry sleeps. This makes the test slower than intended and the comment misleading; expose/configure the backoff or avoid sleeping in unit tests.

The only new consumer tests call `consume()` directly, so they do not exercise the `@KafkaListener` topic/group/deserializer wiring. A Kafka integration test is needed here (as the event-store service has with Testcontainers) to catch misconfigured listener properties or JSON polymorphic deserialization before runtime.

Using `0L` for a missing `occurredAt` anchors the record as 1970-01-01, which corrupts the on-chain audit timestamp for malformed or legacy events. The event-store path falls back to the current time for missing timestamps, so use a consistent non-epoch fallback or reject the event instead of writing an epoch-zero record.

Anchoring a null event type as the literal `UNKNOWN` can create on-chain records that do not correspond to any domain event type and will not match event-store behavior (which requires `event.getEventType().name()`). Reject or fail these malformed events instead of writing ambiguous audit records.

If the consumer thread is interrupted during backoff (for example during application shutdown or container rebalance), this method restores the interrupt flag but the retry loop continues and may still send another blockchain transaction. Treat interruption as a terminal failure so shutdown/rebalance signals are respected.

This documentation describes the JSON as canonical and field-order independent, but the implementation uses the injected Jackson `ObjectMapper` without enabling canonical ordering for properties or map entries. The comment overstates the guarantee; either configure canonical serialization explicitly or adjust the documentation to avoid relying on a property the code does not enforce.

This item is checked as complete even though the implementation is a manually written wrapper and the PR description explicitly says it avoids a Web3j/ABI code-generation step. Rename the subtask to match the implemented approach or keep it unchecked until generated bindings are added.

`@ConditionalOnProperty` only checks that the property is present (and not `false`) by default; because `application.yml` always defines `web3j.private-key` via a placeholder, this bean method is still invoked for an empty key and returns `null`. Returning `null` from a `@Bean` method creates confusing bean metadata; use a condition that actually requires non-blank text instead of relying on a null bean.

These unit tests construct a standalone `ObjectMapper` instead of using the Spring Boot-configured mapper that production injects. Since the hash depends on the exact JSON bytes (including Java time serialization and mapper features), this can miss production-only hash changes; add a Spring-backed test or configure the test mapper identically to the application mapper.

The manual ABI wrapper is not exercised by the new tests because `AuditLedgerContract.load(...)` is statically mocked, so a wrong function signature, parameter order, or ABI type would still pass unit tests and fail only at runtime. Add an integration/encoding test against the deployed AuditLedger ABI or generate the wrapper to reduce this risk.

This comment is misleading: `DuplicateHashException` is only thrown by the local pre-check when `isHashExists` returns true, not when the contract returns a `DuplicateHash` revert from `appendAuditRecord`. Update the comment or add actual revert detection so the documented behavior matches the code.

The retry documentation says contract `DuplicateHash` reverts are treated as successful no-ops, but the code only treats the pre-flight `isHashExists` result that way; a revert thrown by `appendAuditRecord` is handled as a generic failure and retried. Align the documentation with the implementation or explicitly detect duplicate-hash reverts.

This configuration-properties class is registered as a component even though the application enables `@ConfigurationPropertiesScan`. Other property classes in this backend are registered via scan/enable configuration properties rather than `@Component` (for example `command-service`'s `KafkaTopicsProperties`), so this deviates from the established pattern and makes bean registration less consistent.

`isHashExists` is a Solidity `view` function, not `pure` (it reads the `hashExists` mapping). The comment uses a Solidity term with a different meaning, so update it to avoid misleading readers about the contract semantics.

Catching every unexpected `Exception` and returning normally also commits the Kafka offset for defects such as invalid payload handling or misconfigured contract calls. That hides programming/configuration errors and drops the audit event instead of surfacing the failure; restrict the swallowed exception types or route unexpected failures to an error handler/DLQ.

This ObjectMapper produces a different serialized payload than event-store-service, which hashes `objectMapper.writeValueAsString(event)` using `JsonMapper.builder().findAndAddModules().build()` before storing `event_hash` (`EventPersistenceService.java:58-68`). Since the audit writer anchors the hash computed from this sorted serialization, the on-chain hash can differ from the DB `event_hash` for the same Kafka event, breaking integrity verification across the ledger.

`eventId` is not validated even though the event model and event-store schema require a stable non-null event identifier. A malformed Kafka record with `eventId == null` would still be hashed and anchored on-chain, while event-store would reject it because `audit.events.event_id` is NOT NULL, leaving an on-chain hash with no corresponding persisted audit event.

This test name says it covers a missing event type, but the built event also has `occurredAt == null`, so `validateEvent` fails on the timestamp check first and never exercises the `event.getEventType() == null` branch. As a result the missing-event-type behavior is still untested.

The listener container uses the default Spring Kafka error handler, with no DLT/backoff/error-handler configured for blockchain write failures. Throwing from the listener therefore only gets Spring Kafka's finite default retries before the record can be recovered/skipped, so a Ganache outage or missing contract config can still advance the offset without anchoring the event instead of redelivering it later.

The source file is named `AuditEventConsumerKafkaTestcontainersIT.java`, but the test class is named `AuditEventConsumerKafkaTestcontainersTest`. That mismatch makes it unclear whether this Docker-backed test is intended to run as a unit test in Surefire (`*Test`) or as an integration test (`*IT`) and can lead to it being executed in the wrong Maven phase.

The PR description says consumer exceptions are caught and logged so partition processing is never stalled, but this listener lets `anchorEvent` exceptions propagate (and the tests assert rethrowing). Either the description/checklist should be corrected, or the listener/error-handler behavior should be changed to match the stated Kafka partition-safety design.

The manual Web3j wrapper is only covered by mocked service tests and selector assertions; no test sends this transaction against a deployed AuditLedger contract. Because this line is the actual ABI encoding/transaction path, a parameter encoding or Web3j API mismatch would not be caught by the current tests despite the PR claiming Web3j/Testcontainers coverage.

The read-call path is also only indirectly mocked; no test executes this `eth_call` against a real AuditLedger deployment. A mismatch in the boolean return decoding would make idempotency checks fail in production without being caught by the current tests.
`lenient` is already imported by the wildcard static import on the previous line, so this explicit static import is redundant and can be removed to keep the test imports tidy.

This duplicate-revert detection depends on the RPC error message containing the Solidity custom error name. JSON-RPC clients do not standardize revert messages and often return only `execution reverted` plus encoded data, so duplicate races can be treated as retryable failures instead of idempotent success unless the custom error selector/data is decoded or the post-failure existence check is repeated.

The consumer is wired directly to `JsonDeserializer`, so malformed JSON or an untrusted/unknown payload type fails during Kafka polling before the listener can handle it. Spring Kafka recommends `ErrorHandlingDeserializer` for this path; without it, a poison record can keep the partition stuck at the same offset instead of being routed to a recoverer/DLT.

This error handler logs and skips a record after the fixed backoff is exhausted, so a transient Web3/Ganache outage or missing writer config can advance the Kafka offset without anchoring the event on-chain. That creates audit data loss; use a DLT/recoverer with explicit handling or keep the record uncommitted until anchoring succeeds.

This class name is excluded by Surefire's default test includes, and there is no maven-failsafe-plugin configured in the module or parent POM, so this Kafka Testcontainers integration test will not run under the documented `mvn test` command or in a Failsafe phase. Rename it to a Surefire-included pattern or configure Failsafe so the Kafka wiring is actually exercised.

The PR description says consumer exceptions are caught and logged so partition processing is never stalled, but this call intentionally lets exceptions propagate to the container (and the tests assert rethrowing). Please align the description/design decision with the implemented error-handling behavior, or change the consumer if catching is intended.

Starting the listener while this branch throws for missing `web3j.private-key` or `web3j.contract-address` will consume records and, after the configured Kafka retries, publish them to the DLT/commit the original offsets. That means the advertised degraded startup can drain `user.login.events` before the writer is configured, and those events will not be anchored automatically once configuration is added. Consider disabling the listener/readiness until required blockchain config is present, or using a retry policy that does not recover infrastructure-misconfiguration failures to the DLT.

This duplicate-revert fallback is not reliable for the current Solidity contract because `DuplicateHash` is a custom error, so JSON-RPC/Web3j responses often surface only encoded revert data or a generic failed transaction status rather than the literal text `DuplicateHash`. In that race path an idempotent duplicate can be treated as a failed write and sent to retry/DLT; decoding the custom-error selector or rechecking `isHashExists` after a failed append would make the duplicate handling deterministic.

The retry/DLT behavior configured here is not covered by the new Kafka Testcontainers test, which only verifies the successful-consume path. Since failed blockchain writes and poison records are supposed to be retried and then published to `user.login.events.dlt`, add an integration test that forces the listener to fail and asserts the record reaches the DLT; otherwise regressions in the central error-handler wiring would still pass the current suite.

The new hash tests do not assert compatibility with the event-store hash that this service must match; they only check length, determinism, and inequality. A fixed expected digest or a comparison against the event-store `sha256Hex` calculation for the same serialized event would catch ObjectMapper/null-ordering changes that would break cross-ledger integrity while still passing the current tests.

This hard-coded five-minute future cutoff can permanently reject valid audit events from producers with clock skew, delayed replays, or restored fixtures, because the exception is treated as non-recoverable instead of waiting for the timestamp to become acceptable. If the cutoff is required, make it configurable and align the Kafka error-handler classification with whether these events should really go straight to DLT.

This exception is documented as non-recoverable, but the Kafka `DefaultErrorHandler` is not configured to treat it as non-retryable, and the package-private nested type is not accessible from `KafkaListenerConfig` to add that classification. Malformed records will therefore still incur the full Kafka retry/backoff cycle before DLT, contrary to the exception contract; expose/classify it or remove the non-retryable contract.

Routing the DLT record to `record.partition()` requires `user.login.events.dlt` to have at least as many partitions as the source topic. The repo does not provision that topic, so if `user.login.events` is later created with multiple partitions while the DLT is auto-created with the broker default, recovery from higher-numbered partitions will fail and the consumer can get stuck on the poison record; use an unassigned partition or provision/validate matching DLT partitions.

This dereferences the payload before `BlockchainWriterService` can validate it. A valid Kafka tombstone/null value will throw a `NullPointerException` here, so it is treated as an unexpected retryable failure instead of the intended non-recoverable event path.

Classifying `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` as non-retryable means the error handler skips the configured `FixedBackOff`; since the recoverer then rethrows to avoid DLT, an unconfigured service can immediately re-poll the same record and log/fail in a tight loop. Keep a backoff/pausing strategy for this path so degraded startup does not spin the consumer while waiting for configuration.

The future-timestamp rejection path is new behavior but there is no test covering the default tolerance, an overridden tolerance, or the boundary condition. Adding coverage here would help prevent valid clock-skewed events from being incorrectly classified as non-recoverable.

This expected value is produced with the audit-writer `JacksonConfig`, not the event-store mapper, so the test will still pass if event-store serialization changes. It does not actually protect the cross-service hash compatibility described above; use a shared canonical fixture/config or assert against a known event-store-produced JSON/hash.

`@Data` generates a `toString()` that includes `privateKey`, so any accidental logging or diagnostics of this properties bean can expose the signing key. Prefer generating only getters/setters or explicitly excluding/redacting the secret field from `toString()`.

This comment says the mapper is used for Kafka JSON payloads, but the custom producer/consumer factories instantiate `JsonSerializer`/`JsonDeserializer` from class names and do not inject this `ObjectMapper` bean. That makes the documentation misleading; this bean currently controls hash serialization, not Kafka serialization.

This constructor parameter is intended to be optional, but `@Autowired(required = false)` on a single constructor parameter is not the usual nullable-constructor-dependency mechanism and can still leave startup dependent on a `Credentials` bean. Use `Optional<Credentials>`, `ObjectProvider<Credentials>`, or `@Nullable` so the application really starts when `web3j.private-key` is blank.

This null/tombstone branch is unlikely to run because the listener parameter uses `@Payload` with its default `required=true`. Spring Kafka requires `@Payload(required = false)` for tombstone records to be delivered to the method; otherwise argument resolution fails before this check, so the intended `NonRecoverableEventException`/DLT path is bypassed.

This documentation says `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` is treated as non-retryable, but the Kafka error handler intentionally does not register it in `addNotRetryableExceptions` and lets it go through the fixed backoff before the recoverer rethrows it. The comment should match the actual retry behavior to avoid misleading operators/debugging.

This DLT producer is always configured with `JsonSerializer`. For records that fail value deserialization, `ErrorHandlingDeserializer`/`DeadLetterPublishingRecoverer` republishes the original value as `byte[]`; serializing that with `JsonSerializer` changes the payload instead of preserving the original bytes. If poison-record replay/inspection is expected, configure the DLT producer to use a byte-array serializer for `byte[]` values (for example via a delegating serializer) while still using JSON for normal event objects.

This assertion only proves that some record reached the DLT; it does not verify that the dead-lettered record is the event produced by this test or that its payload/key were preserved. Decode or inspect the DLT record (for example by matching the key/eventId) so regressions in DLT routing or serialization are caught.

All non-duplicate contract reverts are treated as transient write failures and eventually routed to the DLT. The deployed `AuditLedger.appendAuditRecord` is `onlyOwner`, so an `Unauthorized` revert from a wrong signer/ownership setup is a configuration problem; draining every event to the DLT in that state loses the intended redelivery behavior. Detect ownership/configuration reverts (or validate ownership before consuming) and handle them like `BlockchainNotConfiguredException`.

`isConfigured()` treats any non-blank contract address as usable. A malformed address, an address with no `AuditLedger` deployed, or a wrong-network address will be handled as write failures, retried, and eventually sent to the DLT even though they are infrastructure/configuration problems. Validate the address/contract (or fail with `BlockchainNotConfiguredException`) so events are not drained to the DLT for configuration typos.

Because the expected hash is recomputed with the same `JacksonConfig` during the test, this does not catch serialization drift for the fixed event; if the mapper configuration changes, both sides change together and the test still passes. Use a literal expected hex value (or a cross-service fixture) so accidental hash-format changes are detected.

This comment overstates the guarantee: random UUIDs are overwhelmingly likely but not guaranteed to differ, and the test already changes `userId` anyway. Reword or remove it to avoid documenting a false assumption.

The list says both exception types bypass the DLT recoverer, but `NonRecoverableEventException` is configured as not-retryable and still goes through the DLT recoverer immediately. This should be reworded so the documented behavior matches the handler configuration.

This configurable tolerance is not constrained to non-negative values. If it is accidentally set below zero, freshly produced valid events will satisfy the future-timestamp check and be classified as non-recoverable, causing them to go to the DLT. Add configuration validation or reject negative values before using the property.

The manual ABI wrapper is not exercised by the added encoding tests; they only recompute selectors from hard-coded signatures. A typo in this `Function` construction (argument order/types or function name constants) could still ship. Add a test that invokes/encodes the wrapper call or runs it against a deployed test contract.

The read-call ABI construction is also only indirectly covered by selector constants, not by exercising this wrapper method or decoding the expected return. A mismatch in output type or function construction would not be caught before runtime.

Creating `Credentials` directly during bean initialization means any non-blank but malformed `web3j.private-key` will throw while the Spring context starts. That bypasses the runtime `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` path described in the service and prevents the advertised degraded startup mode for misconfiguration; consider validating/wrapping this so bad keys are handled consistently at anchor time or fail with a clear configuration error.

This only checks the immediate cause, so a `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` wrapped deeper (for example by `anchorEvent` after retry exhaustion or by listener/container exceptions) will fall through to the DLT recoverer. That breaks the documented тАЬdo not advance offset on blockchain misconfigurationтАЭ behavior; unwrap the full cause chain before deciding whether to publish to the DLT.

`BlockchainNotConfiguredException` thrown from `writeToBlockchain` (for example an `Unauthorized` revert) is caught here as a generic `Exception`, retried, and finally wrapped as `BlockchainWriteException`. That loses the non-DLT configuration signal documented for ownership mismatches and causes unnecessary retries; handle `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` before the generic retry catch.

`Unauthorized` is a Solidity custom error in `AuditLedger.sol`, and Web3j/JSON-RPC often exposes custom errors only as ABI-encoded revert data rather than the literal name. Checking only message text here can miss an owner mismatch, causing it to be treated as a transient write failure instead of a configuration error that keeps the Kafka offset uncommitted.

A negative `web3j.future-timestamp-tolerance-seconds` is an operator configuration error, but this throws a plain `IllegalStateException`. The Kafka error handler will retry and then publish every affected record to the DLT instead of preserving offsets like other blockchain configuration failures; route this through the same non-DLT configuration-error path or validate it at startup.

`isConfigured()` is private and unused, duplicating part of `validateConfiguration()`. Keeping dead configuration checks makes it easier for the two paths to drift; remove it or use it as the single source of truth.
These imports are unused in the test class. Removing them keeps the file clean and avoids IDE/compiler warnings.

This Testcontainers test will fail the Surefire suite on machines where Docker is unavailable, whereas the existing event-store Testcontainers integration test opts into `disabledWithoutDocker = true` to skip cleanly. Add the same flag here so contributors/CI jobs without Docker don't get a hard test failure just from running `mvn test`.

Malformed private keys also take this path because `Web3jConfig` skips creating the `Credentials` bean unless the value matches the 64-hex regex, so the runtime error will incorrectly say the key is missing even when it is present but invalid. Check `props.getPrivateKey()` here and report тАЬmalformedтАЭ separately to make configuration failures diagnosable.

The custom DLT serializer is only needed for deserialization-failure paths where the recoverer republishes raw `byte[]`, but the new Kafka integration test only covers listener exceptions after successful deserialization. Add a malformed-JSON/poison-record test that asserts the raw bytes reach the DLT; otherwise this serializer could regress without any test failing.

This uses the deprecated `org.testcontainers.containers.KafkaContainer` API, while the existing event-store integration test already uses the current `org.testcontainers.kafka.KafkaContainer` package. Switching to the current API avoids deprecation churn when the Testcontainers version is upgraded.

This special recovery path is central to the stated тАЬoffset stays uncommitted and no DLTтАЭ behavior for missing blockchain configuration, but the new Kafka integration test only verifies the normal `BlockchainWriteException` DLT path. Add a test that makes the listener throw `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` and asserts no record is published to the DLT/offset is not advanced, so this custom recoverer behavior is protected.

The transaction receipt is logged as success without checking `receipt.isStatusOK()`. Web3j transaction calls can return a mined receipt with status `0x0` for a reverted transaction (for example `Unauthorized` or another contract revert), so this path can commit the Kafka record even though the hash was not appended on-chain; treat non-OK receipts as failures before logging success.

This test only sets the timestamp one second beyond the 300-second tolerance. If CI pauses for more than about a second between setting `occurredAt` and validation, the event is no longer outside the tolerance and the assertion can fail; use a larger margin or an injectable clock.

This boundary is only one second beyond the configured tolerance, so a short pause before `anchorEvent` runs can make the timestamp fall back within the allowed window and turn the rejection test flaky. Use a larger future offset or control the clock in the service.

A reverted transaction receipt is converted into a generic RuntimeException that does not include the contract revert reason or custom-error selector. For an `Unauthorized()` revert (owner/key mismatch), this bypasses `isOwnershipRevert(...)`, so the error is retried and eventually sent to the DLT instead of being treated as `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` as intended.

This verification can be satisfied by invocations left over from earlier ordered tests because the shared `@MockitoBean` is not reset before this test and the matcher accepts any event. That lets the test start (and even finish) the тАЬno DLTтАЭ assertion before the not-configured record is actually processed, producing false positives; verify the current event id or reset the mock before sending it.

The newly added tombstone branch is not covered by the consumer unit tests or the Kafka integration tests. Since null payloads are intentionally classified as non-recoverable and routed differently by the error handler, add a test that exercises this path so the DLT behavior is protected.

The private-key validity check is duplicated here and in `BlockchainWriterService`, but this condition does not trim the configured value while the runtime check does. A key with accidental surrounding whitespace will skip Credentials bean creation and later be reported as malformed, so consider sharing one validation path (or trimming here) to avoid configuration drift.

This uses the Apache-image `org.testcontainers.kafka.KafkaContainer` with a Confluent `cp-kafka` image by marking it as a compatible substitute. The two images use different startup scripts/env expectations in Testcontainers; when Docker is available this can fail at container startup. Use the Confluent-specific container class for `confluentinc/cp-kafka` or switch the image to an Apache Kafka image.

Events with `occurredAt` before the Unix epoch are not rejected here, but `writeToBlockchain` later encodes the timestamp as a Solidity `uint256`, so those records will deterministically fail ABI encoding after the duplicate check and retry cycles. Validate `getEpochSecond() >= 0` as a non-recoverable event so malformed records go straight to the DLT instead of being treated as transient blockchain failures.

The case-insensitive regex accepts an uppercase `0X` prefix, but Web3j private-key parsing only strips the lowercase `0x` prefix. That means a value this condition treats as valid can still throw during `Credentials.create`, preventing the intended degraded startup path for malformed keys; either normalize the prefix before creating credentials or make the prefix check case-sensitive while still allowing uppercase hex digits.

The zero address passes this format check even though it can never be a deployed `AuditLedger` contract. If `AUDIT_LEDGER_CONTRACT_ADDRESS` is accidentally set to `0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000`, the service treats it as configured and will retry/DLT events as write failures instead of surfacing a configuration error; reject the zero address here as `BlockchainNotConfiguredException`.

This duplicates the event-store hash algorithm and its ObjectMapper assumptions in a separate service. Because DB `event_hash` and on-chain hashes must remain byte-for-byte identical, future changes to either service can silently diverge; consider sharing the canonical event serialization/hash code from a common module or adding a cross-service compatibility test that exercises both implementations.

This ownership-mismatch path relies on `receipt.getRevertReason()`/exception text containing `Unauthorized`, but standard transaction receipts generally only expose status `0x0` and do not include Solidity custom-error data unless Web3j is explicitly configured to extract it. With a non-owner private key, this can be retried and eventually sent to the DLT as a transient write failure, committing the offset, instead of being treated as `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` as documented. Consider checking the contract `owner()` before sending or explicitly extracting revert data for failed receipts.

Embedding the raw private-key property inside a quoted SpEL expression makes degraded startup fragile: a malformed secret containing a single quote or other SpEL-sensitive character can make condition parsing fail during application startup instead of simply skipping the `Credentials` bean, and the resolved secret may appear in condition diagnostics. Avoid interpolating the secret into SpEL; read and validate it in code or a custom condition instead.

This test leaves the shared `audit-writer-consumer` group positioned on an uncommitted record by design. Because the later tests in the same class use the same topic and listener group, they depend on the `@BeforeEach` mock reset racing with the listener redelivery to let this record finally commit; otherwise subsequent poison/tombstone records can be blocked behind this offset and time out. Isolate this scenario with a unique topic/group or stop/seek the listener after the assertion.

The private-key validation regex is duplicated here and in `Web3jConfig`'s `@ConditionalOnExpression`. If one copy changes, startup bean creation and runtime validation can disagree (for example, creating credentials that `validateConfiguration()` later rejects, or vice versa). Centralizing this validation would make the configuration behavior easier to keep consistent.

A null owner response should be treated as a contract/configuration failure, not as permission to continue. For a well-formed but stale or non-contract address (common after a Ganache reset), `owner()` can fail/return no decoded value; this branch then proceeds to `isHashExists`/`appendAuditRecord`, which is handled as a retryable write failure and can send valid events to the DLT instead of keeping the offset uncommitted like other misconfiguration cases.

The owner check runs before the duplicate-hash idempotency check, so a replayed event whose hash is already on-chain will fail as `BlockchainNotConfiguredException` if the signer is no longer the current owner (for example after key rotation). Since `isHashExists` is a view call and duplicates should be treated as success, check for an existing hash before requiring write ownership to avoid blocking the Kafka partition on already-anchored records.

This test hashes a hard-coded `owner()` signature but never exercises `AuditLedgerContract.buildOwnerFunction()` or the `FUNC_OWNER` constant used by the wrapper. A typo in the actual wrapper method would still leave this test passing while `BlockchainWriterService` fails at runtime during the owner check, so add an encoded-call assertion built through the wrapper method.

The private-key check only validates the hex shape, so values such as the all-zero key or numbers outside the secp256k1 private-key range still pass this condition. Because those values then create a Credentials bean, the service either fails during bean creation/signing or drains records through retry/DLT instead of treating the key as misconfiguration; add a numeric range check for 1 <= key < secp256k1 curve order.

If the configured address is syntactically valid but is not the deployed AuditLedger contract, this pre-flight call will throw before the code can classify the address as a configuration problem. That falls through the generic retry path and eventually DLTs records; wrap failed contract-probe calls such as isHashExists/owner as BlockchainNotConfiguredException so offsets are not advanced for a bad contract address.

This test only proves that the not-configured failure is not written to the DLT; it would still pass if the error handler swallowed the exception and committed the source offset. Since the production code relies on this path keeping the offset uncommitted, add an assertion for redelivery or committed offset position for this event.

Because configuration is validated before the event payload, malformed events with missing eventId/occurredAt/eventType are classified as BlockchainNotConfigured whenever the writer is not configured. That keeps the Kafka offset uncommitted instead of sending the non-recoverable record to the DLT, so a bad event can block the partition until Web3j configuration is fixed; validate the event before configuration checks so malformed payloads follow the documented DLT path.

This retry loop is layered under the Kafka DefaultErrorHandler retry configured in KafkaListenerConfig, so a single failing record can execute up to 9 blocking Web3/RPC attempts before being recovered (3 service attempts for each of the initial delivery plus 2 Kafka retries). That amplifies load and delays DLT routing during outages; prefer a single retry layer or make the combined attempt budget explicit/configurable.

The test name describes a signer-not-owner scenario, but the setup never establishes a different owner; the assertion is really that owner() is not probed when the hash already exists. Rename the test to match the behavior it actually verifies, otherwise future readers may think an ownership-mismatch path is covered here.

This recovery log always tells operators to set private-key and contract-address, but BlockchainNotConfiguredException is also used for malformed keys, zero/malformed addresses, owner mismatches, negative timestamp tolerance, and contract probe failures. Include the exception message here so the log points to the actual misconfiguration instead of a generic fix that may already be satisfied.

This comment is misleading: the setting only permits occurredAt values that are ahead of the consumer clock. Delayed replays normally make events older, which this validation already accepts regardless of the tolerance, so the documentation should refer to future-dated fixtures or producer clock skew instead.

This Javadoc says the future-timestamp tolerance helps with delayed replays, but delayed replays produce older timestamps and are not constrained by this check. Clarify that the property is for producer clock skew or intentionally future-dated test fixtures.

The class-level documentation repeats that the future-timestamp tolerance compensates for delayed fixture replays, but replay delay makes occurredAt farther in the past, not the future. This should be limited to clock skew or future-dated fixtures so operators do not tune the wrong setting.

This ObjectMapper configuration is duplicated from event-store-service even though hash compatibility depends on both services serializing events byte-for-byte the same way. A future change to either mapper can break ledger/DB hash verification without a compile-time signal; consider moving the hash ObjectMapper factory to a shared module used by both services.
